mnemonics.title: DP Table Meaning
mnemonics.content: DP: Don't Panic - Table[i][j] = Can I make j using first i elements?

mnemonics.title: Pseudo-polynomial
mnemonics.content: Pseudo means FALSE - polynomial in VALUE but exponential in BITS (size)

mnemonics.title: NP-Complete
mnemonics.content: NPC - Not Polynomial Complete (hard to solve, easy to verify)

mnemonics.title: Backtracking Choice
mnemonics.content: At each element: INclude or EXclude - that's the binary tree

acronyms.expansion: Dynamic Programming - build solution from smaller subproblems
acronyms.usage: Used for exact Subset Sum solution in pseudo-polynomial time

acronyms.expansion: Target Weight/Sum - the value we want to achieve
acronyms.usage: W determines the table size in DP solution

acronyms.expansion: NP-Complete - class of hardest problems in NP
acronyms.usage: Subset Sum is NP-Complete, no poly-time solution known

acronyms.expansion: Meet-in-the-Middle - divide problem into two halves

visualTips.visualization: Think of a grid where rows = elements (0 to n), columns = sums (0 to W). Cell[i][j] = TRUE if sum j achievable using first i items. Fill row by row, left to right.
Binary tree where each level represents one array element. Left branch = exclude, Right branch = include. Leaf nodes represent all possible subsets.
W is the numeric value. If W=1000 (input size ~10 bits), DP takes 1000 operations. But if W=2^1000 (1000 bits), DP takes astronomical time despite small input representation.
Imagine cutting off tree branches when path sum > target. With sorted descending numbers, check if even taking ALL remaining elements can reach target - if not, cut that branch.

analogies.analogy: Like checking if you can pay exact fare using certain coin denominations. Given coins [5,10,15,20] and fare 30, can you make exactly 30? Yes: 10+20 or 15+15 or 5+10+15.
Like solving a jigsaw puzzle (hard to solve) vs. checking if a completed puzzle is correct (easy to verify). Subset Sum is hard to solve but easy to verify a solution.
Reading a book: polynomial in number of pages (n) but exponential in number of words if you count each character as 'input size'. The value matters, not just count.
Splitting a pile of gold coins into two equal-weight piles. It's like dividing a pizza into two equal portions - a special case where target is half total.
Instead of exact change, asking for 'close enough' - if you need 100 and don't have exact, getting 95-100 is acceptable within some tolerance ε.
